headline: Washington builds a tariff around Pix
deck: A new 25% levy reaches machinery, some wood products, ethanol and footwear while sparing beef, coffee, aircraft and other sensitive imports. The legal case stretches from payment rails to the forest edge.
body: The United States began collecting an additional 25% tariff on a broad basket of Brazilian goods at 12:01 a.m. Eastern time on Wednesday. The legal text starts with all Brazilian-origin products and then removes the listed exceptions; USTR’s shorthand is that the levy applies to most imports. Agricultural machinery, some wood products, ethanol and footwear sit among the exposed sectors. [E6][E2][E3]

Exemptions carry much of the macro story. Beef, coffee, orange juice, aircraft and parts, energy products, defined goods already routed through separate sectoral actions, and materials deemed scarce or disruptive were left outside the action. Brazil’s government estimates that goods worth about $7 billion, or 18% of its exports to the United States, face the new duty. The National Confederation of Industry’s estimate reaches $11 billion and 26%. [E2][E3][E6]

That range describes two named assessments, not a newsroom calculation. It also shows the design: Washington can squeeze manufacturers and ethanol producers while limiting the immediate price shock from some of the Brazilian products most visible to US buyers. Footwear exporters have no equivalent shelter; the United States buys one in five pairs that Brazil ships abroad. [E3]

The statute reaches far beyond the goods at the customs gate. USTR’s year-long Section 301 case grouped digital-trade restrictions, electronic payments, preferential tariffs, anti-corruption enforcement, intellectual property, ethanol access and illegal deforestation into one finding of unreasonable practices that burden US commerce. The final tariff then placed leverage on a merchandise basket whose contents do not need to match each allegation item by item. [E1][E2]

Pix is the sharpest mismatch. USTR describes it as a favored national champion that disadvantages competing US payment services. Brazil calls it open public digital infrastructure run to expand instant, low-cost payments, and notes that credit-card use kept growing after Pix launched. The central bank defines the system as an instant-payment rail available around the clock; it is infrastructure over which banks and payment firms compete, rather than an ordinary private wallet. [E2][E4][E5]

Brasília rejects the investigation’s legitimacy under multilateral trade rules. The government says it will start procedures under its Reciprocity Law and return to the World Trade Organization’s dispute system, while continuing consultations with affected industries. It has announced the mechanisms, not a finished retaliatory tariff list. [E4]

The exclusions provide the strongest counter-case to a full trade rupture. US officials remain open to negotiations, Brazil has not yet published counter-duties, and the spared goods reduce the first-round blow. Yet the basket now converts disputes over code, courts, ethanol and forests into a price paid by exporters who may have no part in any of them. [E1][E2][E4]
Pix does not cross the border in a shoe box. Washington has put the tariff on the shoe. [E2][E3]

art.title: The tariff basket
art.caption: The new US duty reaches Brazilian exporters from the industrial southeast to the ports · Map: Graves, Commodities Desk · Terrain: NOAA ETOPO1
